issue_title: has_object_read_permission returning False Still Allows Read

issue_body:
The logging config is about 75% of the way to being useful. Here's a proposal to make it excellent. [This article on logging practices](http://victorlin.me/posts/2012/08/26/good-logging-practice-in-python) has useful contextual advice.

1. Code should log to a logger obtained via `logger.getLogger(\_\_NAME__)`. This results in loggers being named hierarchically after code modules, like `openaddr.conform`. With that naming scheme, logging levels can be configured on a per-module basis.
2. Each module should instantiate the logger once, at the top, via `_L = logger.getLogger(__NAME__)`. That way normal code can just do `_L.debug("foo")`. Note that the logging config has to set `'disable_existing_loggers': False` for this style to work right, see the linked article for details. I'm not wedded to the name `_L`, but I'd like it to be short.
3. There should be a logging setup utility method all the various main() entry points to our code shares. The default behavior is to log warning and above to stderr.
4. The default should be overrideable with a YAML file named `$HOME/.openaddr-machine-logging`, or pointed to by an `OPENADDR_MACHINE_LOGGING` environment variable. 
5. Tests should not log to stderr by default. Maybe make logs write to a file in /tmp that's not deleted if a test fails? Or just rely on the file config for debugging.
6. Mike proposed command line switches like `--verbose` to change the log level. That sounds nice, not sure how it interacts with the YAML file.
As far as Python 3.5 has been released and marked as 'stable' I'd suggest to add rules for it in AppVeyor.

issue_title: Security docs still describe old signature system
issue_body:
We haven't updated the description of the signature system, in `docs/source/security.rst`, since we stored signatures in notebook metadata. They're now stored in an SQLite database. The section on 'collaboration' therefore gives instructions that will no longer work.

Prompted by [this SO question](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/40136923/how-to-enable-shared-trust-in-jupyter-notebook).

issue_title: Automatic kernel-interrupt to avoid OOM kill
issue_body: I don't know how feasible this would be to do, but when using jupyter to run memory-intensive notebooks on machines with limited memory, the python kernel process typically will get killed (when running under linux, by the OOM killer).  The problem with this is that all of the state or prior computation that has been performed by the python kernel is lost.  It would be preferable if Jupyter were able to monitor the memory situation and if a certain limit is reached, to interrupt the running python kernel automatically (not killing it).

issue_title: Subdomain Registrar Registration API should be identical to Domain Registration
issue_body:
When registering a subdomain, a client should be able to POST the same data they would for a domain registration. The only difference in the schema is that a subdomain cannot be a fully qualified address (i.e., cannot contain a TLD).

The response code should also be the same (202) for valid registration attempts, though the message and error codes will be necessarily different.

Move the RESTful API into blockstackd.  There will no longer be a `blockstack api start` command.  Instead, `blockstackd` will run both its back-plane RPC interface and the RESTful interface by default.

We will modify `blockstackd` so it can be started *without* the indexer, in order to preserve the semantics of `blockstack api start`.  It just might look different--probably like `blockstack-core start-api https://url.to.the.indexer.core.node.to.use` for starting just the RESTful interface instead of `blockstack-core start`.

issue_title: is_valid_APK should return True for packed files
issue_body:
is_valid_APK returns True only if the manifest file was parsed and all fields are read.
This is not possible for packed files like Liapp (and probably others).

For this to work, we would need some further changes:
* Use XML Parsers that will not raise errors if the XML is slightly malformed (like empty namespaces)
* Parse the Manifest with best-effort, like extract permissions (as they are usually not packed)
* set some flags that the APK looks packed.

Maybe we can use packer detectors like https://github.com/rednaga/APKiD

We also need to define when an APK is valid. The minimal requirements for an APK are:
* the ZIP file is intact (takes quite long to check)
* The APK has an AndroidManifest.xml (can we decode it?)

That looks like that :
```
target_events_per_sec = 100
tstart = time.time()
while True:
    msg = c.poll(timeout=5.0)
    handle_msg(msg)

    if handled_msg == target_events_per_sec:

issue_title: higher precedence to user assigned positions instead of non-assigned positions
issue_body:
when assigning positions to input field in cwl file, there may occur a case where some inputs are not assigned a position. It seems that all those unassigned fields get 0 position assigned to them. Users usually start assigning position from 1 and thus they come after unassigned fields. Since a user has explicitly mentioned a position, according to me they should get higher precedence than unassigned fields. 
for ex.`cwlVersion: v1.0
class: CommandLineTool
baseCommand: echo
inputs:
  example_flag:
    type: boolean
    inputBinding:
      
      prefix: -f
  example_string:
    type: string
    inputBinding:
      position: 1
      prefix: --example-string
  example_int:
    type: int
    inputBinding:
      
      prefix: -i
      separate: false


outputs: []`

should produce(as per me)
`--example-string hello -f -i42
`
but it produces
`-f -i42 --example-string hello
`
here input file is:
`example_flag: true
example_string: hello
example_int: 42
`
It may be that its a functionality rather than a bug, in that case we would update documentation and mention this behaviour.

issue_title: environment variables set as tmux segments never update value
issue_body:
I tried to set a couple of environment variables as tmux segments. Example:

```
{
  "function": "powerline.segments.common.env.environment",
    "args": {
      "variable": "tnumAll"
    }
 },
```

The variable does show up with the correct value in the correct location when I start a tmux session, but then it never changes even though the environment variable does change. I've confirmed this by typing `echo $tnumAll` and getting the correct value that differs from the one displayed in powerline bar in tmux. I have to close the session and start a new one to see the new values.

By contrast, setting this environment variable as a shell segment works as expected. With every new prompt line, the value displayed is the current one.

I tried looking around for similar reports but didn't find anything, so I'm not sure if this is expected behaviour, or if I'm missing some customization trick, or if it's a bug.

I'm seeing thing with:
powerline: 2.3
tmux: 2.1 (installed with Homebrew)
and iTerm 2.1.4 (latest stable) on OS X 10.11.1

```
(set-info :smt-lib-version 2.6)

(check-sat)

Hence the above should be satisfiable but Z3 thinks it is not.

issue_title: bit2bool/mkbv functions in bitblasted floating-point formulas
issue_body:
_All tests conducted using Z3 4.5.1 64-bit (commit 3350f32e1f2c01c9df63b7d71899796a18ce2272, compiled with gcc  5.4.0) and Python 3 on a Ubuntu 16.04 system._


Hi all,

## Context

I want to "translate" floating-point formulas into CNF boolean formulas using the Z3 API. Here is my first attempt:

```python
from z3 import *

x = FP('x',Float16())
c = FPVal(1024,Float16())
formula = And(x < c, x > 0)
goal = Goal()
goal.add(formula)
# taken from https://stackoverflow.com/a/23932235
tacs = Then(Tactic('simplify'),Tactic('fpa2bv'),Tactic('simplify'),Tactic('bit-blast'),Tactic('tseitin-cnf'))

# track input bits
for i in range(16):
    b = Bool('b' + str(i))
    mapping = b == (Extract(i,i,fpToIEEEBV(x)) == BitVecVal(1,1))
    goal.add(mapping)
    
bitblasted = tacs(goal)[0]
for f in bitblasted:
    print(f)
```

The middle block (`for i in range(16)...`) maps a new boolean to each bit of the input ('x' in this example). The aim is to create a set of variables that, if used as a projection scope for a SAT model counter (e.g. sharpSAT), will make the model counter return the number of solutions for the original formula. 

## Problem

Here is a snippet of the output from the execution of the script above:

``` 
[...]
Or(k!36,
   bit2bool(k!19(mkbv(k!20, k!21,k!22, k!23,k!24,k!25, k!26, k!27, k!28,k!29, k!30, k!31, k!32, k!33,k!34,k!35))),
[...]
```

My expectation was that no functions (well, other than Or/And/Not) would appear in the bitblasted formula. Applying the same idea to bitvector variables directly (example code [here](https://gist.github.com/MateusAraujoBorges/cda62d905e2ae94f2b877450bd2d7478)) works as expected. Am I doing something wrong in the python code above? 

## Addendum

Executing the python code above with `rewriter.hi_fp_unspecified` set to `true` results in a segfault.

issue_title: Move the Menu creation to the main process
issue_body:
As I started working on File -> Open, I started realizing the menu should probably be moved out of the renderer process and into the main process.

One of these reasons is that if you have two windows opened, hitting quit will end up triggering the kernel shutdown and exit for just one window. At least on OS X, when hitting cmd-q, this results in only closing the last window that mutated the Menu.

issue_title: Main process's audio not working when using mpsyt as subprocess
issue_body: I am using google assistant in Ubuntu to play music from Youtube using mpsyt. I use pexpect to run mpsyt and send commands to play music. mpsyt will work but the assistant stops answering or playing any audio. I'm not sure if it is a problem of mpsyt or something else but I never had a similar issue.

#### Problem description

A lot of people use GDAL for uniform gridded data to put it on another grid. GDAL provides a lot of resampling algorithms. It does not work very well with non-uniform spaced data like VIIRS or MODIS which is why I haven't used it for those sensors before. Other sensors like geostationary ones that are already gridded could benefit from these additional resampling algorithms, if for nothing else than to bring in new users.

Last I checked GDAL didn't have all of the gridding API mapped to the python interface, but I'm sure there is a way.

issue_title: Setting up a practical use case starting from this repository
issue_body:
I am very excited that I finally managed to get a Jupyter notebook, using external Spark resources, to run from a Docker container thanks to the work done in this repository. However, now I am struggling to translate this into a practical use case.

I am currently running into a few snags (I have only basic experience using Spark or Docker), and would like to seek help on this list. I would like to handle this step-by-step and not expose all the issues I am facing straight away, to keep this thread clear. (I am not claiming any of these issues have to do with the code base, they are simply due to my lack of expertise.)

My eventual goal is to have a containerized application (either running a Jupyter notebook, or even better, standalone python scripts) that can be deployed on any Spark cluster (e.g. on Azure or Amazon). Furthermore, for testing the way this repository is set up seems ideal. I do not want to setup Azure resources during a testing and debugging phase.

My starting point is a fresh clone of this repository, on which I ran the docker commands to build and run the Spark and Jupyter containers, i.e.
```
git clone https://github.com/jupyter-incubator/sparkmagic.git
docker-compose build
docker-compose up
```
I am now able to run a Jupyter notebook on localhost:8888, and the pyspark3 kernel does its job (I prefer to use Python3).

### Issue 1

Currently, the numpy package is not recognised. Adding the line `RUN pip install numpy` to the file `Dockerfile.jupyter` and rebuilding, does not install the numpy package in the same environment as used by the pyspark3 kernel. 

How can I install package X in my jupyter container?

An interesting observation from running the Jupyter pyspark3 kernel is that I am accessing different environments when using `%%bash` or the os library:
```
%%bash
ls /home
--------
jovyan
```

```
os.listdir("/home")
--------
[]
```

### Issue 2

This is very much related to issue 1. My reason for wanting a Jupyter notebook to run in a container without the need to include an entire Spark installation is to keep the container small and allow for the possibility to run it locally as easily as having it use a remote cluster. It would also be very convenient for workshops and/or demos. 

How can I set up my own Jupyter docker container, containing all the python packages I need so that it can be easily deployed anywhere, using any Spark resources?

With two hosts, like in the default tests configuration, where **test_router_0** is a parent of **test_host_0. On both hosts: `notification_options d,u,r,f,s`, and on the attached contact `host_notification_options d,u,r,f,s`.

When both hosts go down, we get the "DOWN" notification for **test_router_0 and nothing for **test_host_0**. What is expected is to have an UNREACHABLE notification for **test_host_0** ...

The hosts are in the expected states: **test_router_0** is DOWN and **test_host_0** is UNREACHABLE.

Is it a problem dur to the current dependencies being faulty ... not sure. To be checked with more tests ...

issue_title: Missing or empty only_fields defaults to all fields
issue_body:
If you don't include `only_fields` on `Meta` by default all fields will be included on a `DjangoObjectType`.

Sometimes you want to have a `DjangoObjectType` and manually define any fields you need and there is no way to stop this behaviour - I would have assumed `only_fields = ()` would default to no fields, but it doesn't.

As a workaround I've added `only_fields = ('something_that_is_not_a_field_name',)` but I thought I'd raise here in case others were having similar issues.

This is a super useful project and you seem to be fixing bugs which is great. Can you consider auto-releasing to pypi using Travis so we can all get the benefits of the fixes quickly? See https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/deployment/pypi for information.

issue_title: TensorBoard doesn't respect drive names on Windows
issue_body:
Migrated from https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/issues/7856

@enpasos 

> OS: Windows 10 (64 bit)
> TensorFlow Version: 1.0.0
> CUDA: 8.0
> GPU: yes
> 
> Problem: 
> **C**:>tensorboard --logdir=**E**:\tmp\tensorflow\mnist\logs
> => tensorboard starts without loading data (not working and difficult to detect reason)
> 
> **E**:>tensorboard --logdir=**E**:\tmp\tensorflow\mnist\logs
> => tensorboard starts with loading data (works perfectly) 

@mrry

> Unfortunately TensorBoard [uses a colon](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/27a98083a6c16f263d668271889863596efbeb84/tensorflow/tensorboard/backend/application.py#L581) as the separator between the optional "run name" and the path in the `--logdir` flag. That means that `--logdir=E:\tmp\tensorflow\mnist\logs` is interpreted as a run named `E` with path `\tmp\tensorflow\mnist\logs`, which explains why it's sensitive to the current working directory.
> 
> As a quick workaround, you could avoid this problem by always specifying an explicit run name as part of the `--logdir` flag, e.g.:
> 
> ```
> tensorboard --logdir=training:E:\tmp\tensorflow\mnist\logs
> ```
> 
> (Reassigning to @dandelionmane, who shows up on the blame for the relevant code.)

issue_title: TI chips do not report CRC
issue_body:
TI chips do not report the CRC, instead they report status bits in place of the crc.

In sniffer mode (ie: sniffer.py) Wireshark wants a CRC otherwise it reports a BAD FCS

A simple quick workaround is to have the sniffer.py recompute the CRC when given a command line option.

Hey,

I was getting different reported values for geometric mean score when using the geometric_mean_score and was curious why. Looks like there are a few mixed up parameters in classification_report_imbalanced.

geometric_mean_score wants y_true before y_pred:
```
imblearn.metrics.geometric_mean_score(y_true, y_pred, labels=None, pos_label=1, average='multiclass', sample_weight=None, correction=0.0)
````

However, inside classification_report_imbalanced:
```
geo = geometric_mean_score(
            y_pred,
            y_true,
            labels=labels,
            average=None,
            sample_weight=None)
```

So to for index balanced geometric mean:
```
# Index balanced accuracy
    iba_gmean = make_index_balanced_accuracy(
        alpha=alpha, squared=True)(geometric_mean_score)
    iba = iba_gmean(
        y_pred,
        y_true,
        labels=labels,
        average=None,
        sample_weight=sample_weight)
```

On average, the results are quite different:

```
PRE     ++ REC     ++ SPE     ++ F1      ++ GEO     ++ IBA
  0.496 ##   0.485 ##   0.904 ##   0.462 ##   0.637 ##   0.407
```

```
                   pre       rec       spe        f1       geo       iba       sup
...
avg / total       0.50      0.48      0.90      0.46      0.68      0.45      2800
```


After changing param order I was able to get consistent results 

Versions:

issue_title: Proposal: Separate heavy-tests branch, run nightly
issue_body:
Right now the Travis tests are very slow. Most runs today took about 2 hours 46 minutes of wall time. This is mainly due to the macOS tests, which wait in an overcrowded Travis queue for a limited number of macOS instances. Additionally, once they run, they take about 12 minutes apiece. Also, the Boulder integration tests take about 12 minutes, and we run one for each Python version, when we usually only need to run 1.

My proposal is to remove the macOS and all but one of the Boulder integration test jobs from the main Travis build. We would create a separate branch called heavy-tests that would keep these tests in, and configure it as a Travis cron to run nightly. This branch would have an additional before_install step that would merge the latest master, so we would not need to update it manually (except for .travis.yml changes).

issue_title: Make parser understand YAML diagnostics for TAP version 13
issue_body: TAP version 13 added optional YAML blocks to tests. Everything else about the spec stayed the same. If the TAP parser was updated to support YAML blocks, then tappy could be considered version 13 "compliant" (even though YAML blocks never had any formally defined schema).
https://github.com/mozilla-releng/signingscript/pull/37

issue_title: Implement a whitelist for smartcontract code
issue_body:
### Goal

Validate the deployed smart contract's _byte code_.

- Detect if a buggy compiler version is being used and reject it.
- Detect if the smart contract is unknown, reject it because we cannot prove safety.

### White list of curated code

The list is comprised of curated smart contract, each with the corresponding byte code hashes and metadata. The metadata must contain the compiler name/version with additional data to hash the smart contract code (details below).

### Hash

The intent is to white list the smart contract byte code, not the smart contract deploying, for this reason the library addresses must be removed from the byte code. The hashing function must be applied over the *hexadecimal* (without prefixing "0x") representation of the byte code with the libraries addresses replace by zeros.

Steps to produce a white list entry:

- Compile the smart contract with the solc compiler _without_ informing the libraries addresses.
- Replace the addresses with zeros
- Ensure the byte code is hex encode without the leading "0x"
- Hash the result
- The entry must save:
  - The solc version
  - Each library address position
  - The solc flags that change the contract byte code (--optmize and --optmize-runs)
  - The smart contract name

Steps to verify the smart contract:

- On the proxy constructor retrieve the smart contract code through the `eth_getCode` JSON-RPC call.
- Ensure the result is hex encode without the leading "0x"
- For each white list entry of the corresponding contract:
  - Get the addresses positions and replace the content with zeros
  - Hash the result and compare, if equals success!
- If not match is found the supplied address has invalid code, then an exception must be raised.

### TODO

- [ ] Create an initial white list for each smart contract.
- [ ] For each `raiden.network.rpc.client` proxy add the required byte code check.
- [ ] Optionally add a command line flag to ignore the code hashes.

issue_title: Add synchronization for netting channel external state calls
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

ChannelExternalState methods may be called more than once, e.g.:

- Two concurrent tasks are running against a node that goes offline
- Once each task reaches the unsafe region, bot will emit the ContractSendChannelClose
- Without some sort of check, close will be called twice

## Solution

Flag the ChannelExternalState for "waiting_for_close", and only call `close` once.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Write a test
- [ ] Fix the problem

issue_title: Force HTTPS for the REST endpoints
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Raiden cannot expose the REST endpoints to non-local IPs without encryption.

## Solution

Use [Flask-SSLify](https://github.com/kennethreitz/flask-sslify) and generate the certificates on the first startup.


## Tasklist

- [ ] Generate the certificate
- [ ] SSLify

## Discussion

An alternative is to never expose the Raiden endpoints to external IP addresses or require to user to use a front-end server (e.g. nginx).

issue_title: Automatic preview of side panel expressions
issue_body:
I frequently select variables and send them to side panel to view their values (C-S-Enter). The expressions will be evaluated and displayed but 

1. I do not know the length of array (or size of dataframe) 
2. for assignment I sometimes need to know the assigned value

It would therefore make sense to automatically append a preview statement in such cases. E.g. convert

```
my_var
```

to 

```
%preview my_var
```

and 

```
my_var = some_expression
```

to 

```
%preview my_var
my_var = some_expression
```

I'm not sure if this request makes sense but in my case I can either run a script locally or remotely as the two computers I have are identical. One can of course comment out the specs in `task` when local execution is desired, but it can be a pain if there are many of them. Would a global switch help? Or some other switches that can finer control if remote execution is to be activated for given tasks. We can discuss if this request is reasonable then the interface.

issue_title: FFmpeg vs. Streamlink: Output file size
issue_body:
I am able to successfully download a HLS stream from a .m3u8 file on both FFmpeg and Streamlink. However, the resulting files are of different sizes. The weird thing is that that when analyzing the elementary streams through both MediaInfo and FFmpeg, they are exactly the same. So I hypothesized that the muxing process must be different between the programs.

So, in that case I put my theory to the test. Since Streamlink uses FFmpeg as a muxer, if I were to use the same version for each they would come out identical right? Well, I downloaded the most recent build and linked a path to it in the config file. The outcome was the exact same.

So if anyone can help me I'd greatly appreciate it. I'd just like an explanation as to why the elementary streams are identical, however even when using the same build of FFmpeg within each program I get different file sizes. Thanks.

issue_title: How bind all traffic to specific interface ip
issue_body:
My first need is this, specify the IP interface through which the traffic must pass, trying to solve this question came many others.

Correct me if I'm wrong, this functionality does not exist, then my first idea was to create a custom proxy where I would spend the interface ip on the link and pass the proxy by command line --http(s)-proxy, ex:
http://127.0.0.1:8080/proxy/10.0.0.1/http(s)/

I expected the livestreamer call something like:
http://127.0.0.1:8080/proxy/10.0.0.1/http(s)/my.requested.link.com/streaming/playlist.m3u8

But that's not what happened, it ignores all directories and I get something like this:
http://127.0.0.1:8080/http(s)://my.requested.link.com/streaming/playlist.m3u8

Ie is totally impractical to use the proxy command to try to pass the traffic through a specific interface.

The second idea would be to pass the link .m3u8 directly with the proxy link, but would not work for other livestremer plugins and also not work with hls streams where each segment has the full file path and not just the name.

Stripe 1.6.1 doesn't seem very happy in a Windows 7, Python 2.7.2 environment. If you simply run:

>>> import stripe
>>> stripe.VERSION

You'll get: AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'VERSION'

You'll receive the same error when trying to use any of the other classes as well, like Token, Customer, Charge, etc.
